metadata.seed: Sometimes I can’t help but feel this void despite being with people who are close friends and family. I don't know if I am being ungrateful, but I don't feel like I have anyone to talk to when things get hard. I don't belong anywhere. I just don't want to wake up ever again.

patient.query: Sometimes I can’t help but feel this void despite being with people who are close friends and family. I don't know if I am being ungrateful, but I don't feel like I have anyone to talk to when things get hard. I don't belong anywhere. I just don't want to wake up ever again.

triggers: being with people who are close friends and family
automatic_thoughts: I don't have anyone to talk to
I don't belong anywhere
I just don't want to wake up ever again

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a deep sense of emptiness and isolation, even when you’re surrounded by people who care about you. That must feel exhausting and overwhelming. You deserve help—have you thought about reaching out to a trusted friend, family member, or counselor to share how hard this feels?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel really exhausting. I often find myself feeling like I’m on the outside looking in, even when I’m with others. I know I should reach out to someone, but I just... I don’t want to burden them, you know? It's like I keep convincing myself that it’s better to just keep it all in.

automatic_thoughts: I’m on the outside looking in
I don’t want to burden them
it’s better to just keep it all in

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds exhausting to feel like you’re on the outside, especially when you’re with people. What evidence do you have that sharing your feelings would actually burden them? Have there been moments when opening up felt helpful or even welcomed?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Fear of being a burden to others
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: It feels exhausting. I often feel like I’m on the outside looking in, even when I'm with others. I should reach out, but I don't want to burden anyone. I keep convincing myself it’s better to just keep it all in.

automatic_thoughts: I’m on the outside looking in
I should reach out
I don't want to burden anyone
it’s better to just keep it all in

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds exhausting to feel like you’re on the outside looking in, even when you’re with people. What evidence do you have that reaching out would make others feel burdened? Have there been moments when sharing something didn’t feel like a weight on anyone else?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Fear of being a burden to others
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: I guess... I just remember times when I’ve shared and it felt like I was bringing everyone down. It’s like I can hear the silence afterward or see their expressions change, and it makes me think it’s better to just stay quiet. But, there have been a couple of times when it felt okay, like the person really listened and cared. It just... feels so rare.

automatic_thoughts: it’s better to just stay quiet
I was bringing everyone down

neutral: Fear of being in a small group (finding)
Tends to avoid group social interactions (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’ve noticed a pattern where sharing feels like it pulls the group down, but you’ve also had a few moments where someone really listened. What evidence do you have that your contributions are always negative, and how might you weigh the times when people responded positively? Could we think about what might happen if you tried sharing again, but with a slightly different approach or in a smaller setting?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Fear of saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Tends to avoid group social interactions
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: I guess I’ve just had more of those negative experiences stick with me. It’s hard to shake off the times when I felt like I was a downer. But I can remember a couple of moments where people actually seemed to care, and it felt... nice, I guess? Trying again feels scary, though. What if it backfires, you know? I don’t really know how to

triggers: times when I felt like a downer
automatic_thoughts: I guess I’ve just had more of those negative experiences stick with me
what if it backfires

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
contradiction: Enduring personality change after catastrophic experience (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that past negative moments will keep defining you and that trying again might fail. What evidence do you have that those negative experiences will always stay with you? Are there times when you’ve bounced back or found support? How might you test whether trying again could actually lead to something positive?

retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Tends to avoid group social interactions
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel like those moments have a stronger hold on me. It's like they replay in my head all the time. I mean, I’ve had some ups and downs, and there have been times I’ve felt a little stronger or more connected. I guess I have to remind myself that not every experience is the same. Testing it, though... it feels risky. What if it goes

automatic_thoughts: not every experience is the same
what if it goes

neutral: Enduring personality change after catastrophic experience (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of tension around the idea of confronting those memories. What evidence do you have that testing this will lead to something negative? And how have you managed similar worries in the past?

patient.query: Yeah, I think there's a lot of tension around that. The memories just pop up, you know? I remember feeling shut down or brushed off, and it sticks with me. I guess I’ve managed by just avoiding situations where I’d have to open up. It's like I’ve built this little wall around myself, thinking it’s safer that way. But then I just end up feeling more isolated

triggers: situations where I’d have to open up
automatic_thoughts: I remember feeling shut down or brushed off
I’ve built this little wall around myself
it’s safer that way

entailment: Fear of being left alone during period of dependence (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel that keeping a wall around yourself is the safest way to protect yourself. What evidence do you have that opening up would actually be unsafe, and what evidence might suggest it could be safe or even helpful? Exploring both sides could help you see if there’s a different way to feel secure without feeling isolated.

patient.query: I guess I can see both sides. I mean, the times I’ve opened up, it hasn’t always gone badly. There were those moments where I felt heard, and that was nice. But then, there’s this voice in my head that reminds me of the times it felt like I was just too much, or that people didn’t really want to deal with my issues. It’s like

automatic_thoughts: I was just too much
people didn’t really want to deal with my issues

entailment: Able to recognize own emotional pain and distress (finding)
Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’ve had both supportive and challenging moments when you open up. What specific experiences make you feel like you’re “too much,” and what evidence do you have that people actually don’t want to deal with your issues? Exploring those details might help you see if the voice is reflecting a pattern or just a single perspective.

patient.query: I guess one time, I shared something pretty personal with a friend, and their reaction was kind of... distant. They changed the subject quickly, and I felt really embarrassed afterward. It made me think that maybe I just shouldn’t have said anything at all. But then there are those moments where a stranger or someone I didn’t expect is genuinely kind and patient, and it catches me off guard.

automatic_thoughts: I shouldn’t have said anything at all

entailment: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you felt embarrassed after sharing, and you’re questioning whether you should have kept it to yourself. What evidence do you have that sharing personal things always leads to a distant reaction? Are there moments when sharing felt safe or was received positively?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: Yeah, that’s true. I think I just focus more on the times it didn’t go well. It’s like that one moment overshadows all the good ones. There have been times, like with my sister, where she really listened and supported me, and it felt like a weight lifted. But then, I worry that I’ll only remember the negative and not give myself a chance to

automatic_thoughts: I focus more on the times it didn’t go well
That one moment overshadows all the good ones
I’ll only remember the negative

neutral: Enduring personality change after catastrophic experience (finding)

llm_response.response: Do you have specific examples where you feel you only remember the negative? It might help to look at moments when you actually recalled the supportive times with your sister. By weighing both sides, you can see that the positive moments aren’t completely eclipsed. How might you remind yourself of those good experiences when the negative ones start to dominate?
